sections.title: Definition of banking business and related terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Banking Act, including 'close relative', 'controller', 'debentures', and 'securities'. It specifies that all business activities constitute a single business for regulatory purposes and provides meanings for terms referenced in other sections. The definitions are critical for understanding the scope of banking activities and regulatory obligations under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines terms relevant to banking operations, which may impact how data is classified and handled across borders for financial institutions.

requirements.text: All activities carried on by a person by way of business are regarded as a single business
Close relative includes spouse, parent, remoter lineal ancestor, step-parent, son, daughter, remoter issue, stepson, stepdaughter, brother or sister
Controller means a 12% controller or 20% controller as defined in section 15B(3)
Debentures have the meaning given by section 4(1) of the Companies Act 1967
Securities have the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Securities and Futures Act 2001

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of subsection (7), all the activities which a person carries on by way of business is to be regarded as a single business carried on by the person.

sections.title: Capital adequacy requirements for Singapore banks
sections.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore may require banks to maintain capital funds in Singapore based on risk assessments. Banks must comply with capital adequacy requirements through written notices, and failure to comply results in fines up to $250,000. The Authority can vary requirements based on risk factors and impose restrictions on distributions if capital thresholds are not met.
sections.dtia_summary: Capital adequacy requirements affect cross-border data transfers by mandating Singapore-based banks to maintain local capital reserves, which impacts data processing and storage within Singapore's financial sector.

sections.source_quote: The Authority may, by written notice, require any bank in Singapore or class of banks in Singapore to maintain capital funds in Singapore of such amount (not being less than the minimum prescribed in section 9 or 9A, as the case may be) and in such manner as the Authority considers appropriate, having regard to the risks arising from the activities of the bank or class of banks (as the case may be) and such other factors as the Authority considers relevant.

sections.title: Bank mergers require approval and public notice
sections.summary: Banks must obtain Ministerial approval for mergers meeting specific conditions: the merger must involve wholly-owned subsidiaries, include a signed merger agreement, and the application must be submitted within two weeks of the agreement. Approved mergers require publication of a notice in four local languages within one week of approval. The Companies Act 1967 does not apply to banks involved in these mergers.

sections.title: Defences for shareholding contraventions in banking
sections.summary: This section outlines defences for individuals or entities charged with contravening shareholding or voting power regulations in Singaporean banks. It specifies that a person may claim ignorance of the contravention and that they notified the Minister within 14 days to take corrective actions, provided they acted within the timeframe set by the Minister. The section also details conditions under which a contravention is not a defence if the person intended to contravene the law.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which shareholding activities may be defended in the context of banking regulations, relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border shareholding structures and compliance with Singapore's banking authority.

sections.summary: The Authority may conduct secret investigations into Singapore banks' books if it suspects they are harming depositors, insolvent, or violating the Banking Act. sections.title: Merchant bank reporting on unfit persons and business risks
sections.summary: A Singapore merchant bank must immediately inform the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) if it becomes aware that a specified person (a substantial shareholder or indirect controller) is no longer fit and proper per the Guidelines on Fit and Proper Criteria, or if the bank is unlikely to conduct business prudently. If MAS determines a specified person is unfit, it may direct the transfer or disposal of shares held by that person, restrict transfers, or impose other directions. These restrictions apply until the transfer is effected or the restriction is revoked, preventing voting rights, share issuance, and dividend payments without MAS authorization. The provisions override the Companies Act 1967 and the merchant bank's articles of association.

This impacts data transfers involving shareholder information across borders.

sections.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) may revoke a merchant bank's license if the bank has ceased all permitted business, provided false information, has had its license withdrawn by a supervisory authority abroad, has made arrangements with creditors, has insufficient assets, is contravening the Act, or has been convicted of an offense. MAS must give written notice and require the bank to show cause before revocation. The bank has 21 days to appeal to the Minister, and the revocation does not take effect until confirmed or the appeal is dismissed. The appeal does not affect other powers MAS or the Minister may exercise.
